plan: Step 1: Add a shared helper in spatialGradient.ts — export a new function `computeVibrantVertexColors(positions: Float32Array, seed: string, axis?: 'x' | 'z' | 'radial')` that internally calls `createSpatialGradient(..., { mode: 'vibrant' })` then `computeVertexColors()`. This gives every mode a single call to get vibrant gradient colors from positions. Terrain can also be refactored to use this helper.

Step 2: Add `aVertexColor` attribute + `vVertexColor` varying + `uHasVertexColor` uniform to the vertex shaders of all 5 point/line-based modes. Files: particleWarp.vert.glsl, pointWarp.vert.glsl, crystalWarp.vert.glsl, ribbonWarp.vert.glsl, flowRibbon.vert.glsl. In each vertex shader, add `attribute vec3 aVertexColor; varying vec3 vVertexColor;` and set `vVertexColor = aVertexColor;` alongside existing color computation.

Step 3: In the fragment shaders of particles, pointcloud, crystal, ribbon, flowribbon — add `uniform float uHasVertexColor; varying vec3 vVertexColor;` and replace the direct use of `vColor` with `vec3 baseColor = mix(vColor, vVertexColor, uHasVertexColor);` before passing to chromaticPoint/chromaticLine. This mirrors the terrain pattern. Files: particleWarp.frag.glsl, pointWarp.frag.glsl, crystalWarp.frag.glsl, ribbonWarp.frag.glsl, flowRibbon.frag.glsl.

Step 4: In each system's TypeScript init() function (particleField.ts, pointCloud.ts, crystalField.ts, ribbonField.ts, flowRibbonField.ts), import and call `computeVibrantVertexColors()` with the positions array to produce vertex colors, then attach as `geometry.setAttribute('aVertexColor', new BufferAttribute(colors, 3))`. Add `uHasVertexColor: { value: 1.0 }` to each mode's uniforms. Remove the old HSL-based per-vertex color initialization loop (the `color.setHSL(...)` + `colorsArr[...]` pattern).

Step 5: For fractalGrowthWireframe (wireframe + vertex-dot shaders) — this mode has no per-vertex positions at init time that map well to a spatial gradient (it generates geometry procedurally). Two sub-approaches: (a) For wireframe.frag.glsl and wireframeVertex.frag.glsl, replace the HSL color computation with a GLSL-side tri-stop gradient lookup using the vertex's normalized spatial position. Add a `triStopGradient(float t)` GLSL function that interpolates between the 4 vibrant reference stops (deep blue → purple → magenta → orange) in linear space. (b) Pass the 4 gradient stop colors as uniforms (vec3 uGradStop0..3) so the palette stays seed-perturbed and consistent with other modes.

Step 6: Clamp lightness values in vibrant vertex colors to prevent bloom clipping. The vibrant palette's orange stop (#ff7a2a) has high luminance. Ensure the gradient stops are authored in linear space (already done in createVibrantGradient) and keep final fragment color values ≤ 0.85 luminance pre-bloom. In each fragment shader, after computing the final `color` but before `gl_FragColor`, add a soft luminance cap: `float lum = dot(color, vec3(0.299, 0.587, 0.114)); color *= min(1.0, 0.85 / max(lum, 0.001));` — this prevents any color from exceeding 0.85 luminance, which with bloomThreshold=0.4 and bloomStrength≤1.2 will avoid clipping to white.

Step 7: Remove dead HSL per-vertex color code. Delete the `colorsArr` array creation, the `color.setHSL(...)` loops, and the `geometry.setAttribute('color', ...)` calls from the 5 point/line system files. The `aHueOffset` attribute and `hueOffsetsArr` can also be removed from these 5 systems since the vibrant gradient replaces hue-offset-based coloring. Remove `hsl2rgb` from vertex shaders that no longer compute HSL colors (particles, ribbon, pointcloud, crystal, flowribbon vertex shaders). Keep `hsl2rgb` in wireframe/terrain fragment shaders as fallback path.

Step 8: Update terrain's init to use the new shared helper `computeVibrantVertexColors()` instead of calling `createSpatialGradient` + `computeVertexColors` directly — keeps all modes using the same code path.

Step 9: Write/update tests. (a) Unit test: verify `computeVibrantVertexColors` returns expected color values for known positions/seed. (b) Integration test per mode: instantiate each system, verify `aVertexColor` attribute exists on geometry with correct length and all finite values. (c) Snapshot test: verify no shader compile errors for all 7 modes (existing shader compile tests should cover this — extend if needed). (d) Visual regression: manual check that bloom doesn't clip colors to white on high-quality tier.

Step 10: Build verification — run `npm run build` and `npm run dev`, open in browser, cycle through all modes, confirm: vibrant gradient visible on all modes, no console errors, stable first frame, bloom doesn't wash out to white.
testing_recommendations: Run `npm run build` — must pass with zero errors
Open dev server, let mode rotation cycle through all 7 modes — each should show the deep-blue → purple → magenta → orange gradient spatially across the geometry
Check browser console for shader compile errors (VALIDATE_STATUS, invalid program) on every mode transition
Verify first-frame stability: no black flashes or missing geometry during mode transitions
Test with bloom enabled (high quality tier): confirm no color channels clip to pure white — the orange end of the gradient is the highest risk
Test with bloom disabled (low quality tier): confirm colors are still vibrant and visible
Verify fractalGrowth wireframe mode shows spatial color variation (not a flat single hue)
Run existing test suite: `npm test` — all existing spatial gradient and shader tests must pass
Test with `prefers-reduced-motion` enabled — colors should be unaffected (this is a palette-only change)
Verify muted audio still drives treble/bass reactivity on the new palette colors
risks: Shader compile errors from adding new attributes/varyings/uniforms — each of the 10+ shader files must have matching declarations between vert and frag stages

FractalGrowth wireframe mode has no pre-computed vertex positions for spatial gradient sampling — needs a GLSL-side gradient or uniform-based approach, which is a different pattern from the other 6 modes

Bloom clipping on the orange gradient stop (#ff7a2a → high luminance in linear space) — the soft luminance cap must be tuned against bloomStrength=1.2 / bloomThreshold=0.4 to avoid both clipping and dimming the palette
Removing aHueOffset attribute from 5 vertex shaders is a breaking change if any draw() method still updates it — must verify no runtime references remain

The vibrant gradient is computed from positions at init time — modes that animate positions heavily (particles, flowribbon) will have colors based on initial positions, not current positions. This matches terrain's approach but is worth noting.
Compound modes (two systems layered) may have visual color conflicts if both layers show the same gradient — may need per-mode axis variation (x vs z vs radial) to avoid identical color banding
